topic: A person learns their childhood bully became a therapist
raw_output:
[story 1]

The polished oak of Dr. Elias Thorne’s office seemed to absorb all the light, leaving the waiting room in a perpetual, muted twilight. I sat rigidly on the edge of the uncomfortable leather chair, my knuckles white where I gripped the worn strap of my messenger bag. When he finally looked up from the sheaf of papers before him—his eyes, those same sharp, assessing eyes that used to catalog my every stumble in the hallway—a cold knot tightened in my chest. He offered a small, practiced smile, the kind that suggested deep understanding, which only amplified the sheer wrongness of the situation. "Come in, Leo," he murmured, his voice smooth as aged whiskey. I swallowed, the dryness in my throat making the effort painful. Every instinct screamed at me to bolt, to run back to the familiar sting of confrontation, but the air in this room felt heavy, suffocatingly controlled.

[story 2]

I kept catching myself watching the way he moved. It wasn't the swagger I remembered from middle school; it was deliberate, measured, almost surgical. He leaned forward, resting his forearms on the desk, and the familiar shadow of his presence washed over me. My breath hitched, shallow and quick. "So, tell me about the anxiety," he prompted, his tone entirely devoid of malice, yet somehow infinitely more invasive than any taunt ever had been. I shifted my weight, my gaze darting toward the window, focusing on the distant, indifferent cityscape. My hands began to tremble, a faint vibration that I tried to suppress by pressing my fingernails into my palms. It felt like standing too close to a live wire, waiting for the inevitable spark.

[story 3]

It started with a misplaced file, a casual glance across the reception desk, and then the realization hit me like a physical blow. There he was, the architect of my worst memories, now presiding over a room filled with fragile confessions. When he finally acknowledged my presence, his expression was one of detached professional concern, a mask so flawlessly applied it was terrifying. I couldn't meet his eyes; instead, I focused intently on the pattern of the expensive rug beneath my feet, counting the fibers, trying to anchor myself against the dizzying sense of being utterly exposed. A low hum of unease resonated deep in my core, a feeling of being fundamentally misplaced in this sterile, therapeutic space.
